query: sensitivity of serum ferritin vs serum iron in detecting iron depletion

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Iron profile: This profile includes serum iron, ferritin, and total iron-binding capacity, which help assess iron stores and iron utilization.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Hereditary Hemochromatosis -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Total serum iron and total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) assays can be used in conjunction with ferritin assays to aid in diagnosing iron deficiency or overload. No significant differences in apparent concentration have been reported between serum and plasma samples for total iron determination.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Hereditary Hemochromatosis -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: The serum ferritin concentration and serum transferrin saturation (TSAT) are increased in patients with HH. Further testing should be considered if the ferritin levels exceed 200 µg/L in menstruating females or 300 µg/L in males or nonmenstruating females, or if the TSAT is >45%. [1] TIBC values >450 mcg/dL (80.55 mmol/L) can also help diagnose pathological iron accumulation. [25] The negative predictive value for iron overload is 97% when serum ferritin is normal and TSAT <45%. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia Screening -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Soluble transferrin receptor: This test differentiates between IDA and anemia of chronic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Anemia -- Evaluation -- Differentiation of microcytic anemias based on iron studies
retrieved_docs.content: Iron deficiency anemia: Low serum iron, high TIBC, and low ferritin. Anemia of chronic disease: Low serum iron, low TIBC, and high ferritin. Sideroblastic anemia: High serum iron, normal TIBC, and high ferritin. Thalassemia: Normal serum iron, normal TIBC, and normal ferritin.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The following reference ranges for iron metabolism parameters valuable guidance for assessing iron status and diagnosing related disorders. Iron-binding capacity: 255 to 450 mcg/dL. Transferrin-iron saturation percentage: 25% to 35%. UIBC: normal values for UIBC may vary among laboratories, but most laboratories define their normal range as 111 to 343 mcg/dL. TIBC: normal values vary among laboratories, generally 240 to 450 mcg/dL. [22] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: TSAT is calculated as the ratio of serum iron to TIBC. It is one of the earliest biomarkers of iron deficiency and is useful when serum ferritin is unequivocal. A decrease (less than 20%) indicates an iron deficiency, either absolute or functional. [15] [3] Hemoglobin does not become low until a significant percentage of body iron becomes depleted. Hence, normal hemoglobin does not exclude iron deficiency. Other iron studies available for the evaluation of iron deficiency are:

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Deficiency Anemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory evaluation will identify anemia. The hemoglobin indices in iron deficiency will demonstrate a low mean corpuscular hemoglobin and mean corpuscular hemoglobin volume. Hematoscopy shows microcytosis, hypochromia, and anisocytosis, as reflected by a red cell distribution width higher than the reference range. Serum levels of ferritin, iron, and transferrin saturation will be decreased. Serum ferritin is a measure of the total body iron stores. The total iron-binding capacity will be increased. Stool for occult blood may reveal a gastrointestinal source of bleeding. A simple mean corpuscular hemoglobin/RBC index, or Mentzer index, can help differentiate between the two causes of microcytic/hypochromic anemia. These causes are iron deficiency and thalassemia minor. An index greater than 15 suggests iron deficiency, while an index less than 11 suggests thalassemia minor. The definitive test to rule out thalassemia minor is hemoglobin electrophoresis. Other tests like an iron profile are necessary for severe anemia or when anemia does not respond to iron therapy. Low ferritin is a reliable marker of iron deficiency. However, a ferritin level that is within the reference range or elevated is not very useful in patients with inflammatory conditions such as malignancies, infection, and collagen disease. This is because it is an acute-phase reactant. The standard for establishing iron deficiency is a bone marrow aspiration or biopsy followed by iron staining since it is unaffected by inflammation. However, the cost and invasiveness of this test make it less feasible; it is rarely performed for this reason. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: An ion-exchange technique may be employed to measure serum iron. In this method, a serum sample is added to an aliquot of ion-exchange resin preloaded with iron. Following a short period of equilibration, the serum sample (transferrin) is saturated with iron; the affinity of iron for the serum transferrin is greater compared to the affinity for the resin. An aliquot of this iron-saturated serum is analyzed using the same method as total iron analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemochromatosis -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of hemochromatosis begins with serum transferrin saturation or serum ferritin concentration testing. [20] The mainstay of diagnosis requires a transferrin saturation greater than 45% or a serum ferritin greater than 200 µg/L in females or 300 μg/L in males. [2] Transferrin saturation testing in erythropoietic hemochromatosis may not be as effective in testing for iron overload in these patients. Ferritin specificity can be affected by inflammatory conditions. Ferritin is a "phase reactive protein," and in conditions of inflammation, indicated by an increase in either the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) or C-reactive protein (CRP), ferritin may appear elevated, giving a false level of concentration. Ferritin levels above 200 mcg/L in women or 300 mcg/L in men or a transferrin saturation of >40% in women or 50% in men should lead to further testing.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Transferrin iron saturation can be calculated using the total serum iron and TIBC or transferrin measurement. Transferrin concentration can indirectly derive the TIBC and transferrin iron saturation. [32] Theoretically derived formulas for these calculations are as follows: 1 mol transferrin is 79,570 Da with the capacity to bind 2 atoms of iron with an atomic mass of 55.84 Da, therefore: Transferrin (g/L) = 0.007 × TIBC (μg/L), and TIBC (μg/dL) = transferrin (mg/dL) × [1.41 x TIBC (μmol/L)], or = transferrin (g/L) × 25.2 Transferrin saturation (TSAT %) = [serum Fe (μg/dL) / transferrin (mg/dL)] × 70.9 = [serum Fe (umol/L) / transferrin (mg/dL)] × 398 [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Hepcidin -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Serum iron studies are useful to evaluate the status of iron homeostasis in the body. This panel of blood tests typically includes serum iron, transferrin, or total iron-binding capacity (TIBC), ferritin, and the percentage of transferrin saturation. A level of urinary excreted hepcidin can also be measured. A complete blood count (CBC) may be used to evaluate signs of anemia. Serum Iron: circulating iron with a normal range of 65 to 175 mcg/dL in males and 50 to 170 mcg/dL in females Ferritin: predominantly an intracellular iron storage molecule, serum ferritin directly correlates to total body iron stores - the normal range is 20 to 250 mcg/L in males and 10 to 120 mcg/L in females Transferrin or total iron-binding capacity: a measure of transferrin molecules available to bind iron - TIBC is an indirect measurement Transferrin saturation: a calculated measurement that reflects the amount of bound serum iron using the equation: serum iron divided by TIBC

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Testing -- Iron
retrieved_docs.content: Iron is tested via serum-based indicators of iron status, such as hemoglobin, plasma ferritin, and plasma transferrin saturation. Hemoglobin is routinely measured to indicate anemia but is not specific for iron. Plasma ferritin is the gold standard of iron status because it is the most specific indicator of iron stores. A plasma ferritin concentration of less than 20 ng/L indicates iron deficiency. High plasma ferritin values can indicate iron overload. Transferrin saturation indicates the ratio of iron to transferrin to reflect transport iron. Transferrin saturation is low-cost but shows a pronounced diurnal variation. [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Beta Thalassemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Iron Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Normal or slightly increased ferritin levels are present in thalassemia. Transferrin levels are almost normal in thalassemia as compared to iron deficiency anemia. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Iron studies: Serum ferritin and transferrin levels can differentiate thalassemia from IDA. A normal or slightly increased ferritin level with nearly normal transferrin is more consistent with thalassemia than IDA, which often manifests with low ferritin and elevated transferrin.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: A practical and chemical method for determining TIBC in serum or plasma was first reported in 1957, refined in 1978, and revised in 1990. [29] However, in the 1990s, more direct TIBC assays were developed. Most automated chemistry analyzers measure UIBC and calculate TIBC. Transferrin measurements have mostly replaced testing for UIBC and TIBC outside the United States. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1349
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol is classified as a hematinic, indicating that it belongs to the group of agents used to increase hemoglobin levels and treat iron‑deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: provides multiple iron salts (ferrous fumarate, polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous cysteine glycinate) together with vitamin C derivatives (ascorbic acid, magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate) that facilitate iron uptake, and a spectrum of B‑vitamins and cofactors that support erythropoiesis and overall metabolic health. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how specific oral iron preparations—whether bivalent iron‑ascorbate salts or broader multivitamin‑iron combinations—are organized within therapeutic classes and formulated to maximize iron bioavailability while addressing concurrent micronutrient deficiencies commonly seen in patients with iron deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2809
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with several obstetric conditions, including gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل), threatened miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد). These associations highlight the importance of evaluating and managing metabolic, hematologic, and early‑pregnancy complications when counseling patients about elective pregnancy termination, ensuring comprehensive risk assessment and optimal maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations represent a therapeutic class of iron‑based agents used to treat iron‑deficiency anemia, and this class includes specific compounds such as ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate. Both ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate are members of the iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations group, providing clinicians with oral options to replenish deficient iron stores in patients. Understanding that these individual agents belong to the same antianemic class helps guide appropriate selection and dosing in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE is a pharmaceutical preparation that combines iron with folic acid and is classified as a member of the iron‑in‑combination‑with‑folic‑acid group, reflecting its role in providing both elemental iron and folate for the treatment of iron deficiency and support of folate-dependent metabolic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 49
retrieved_docs.content: anemia (Arabic: فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات), reflecting the well‑established relationship that folate deficiency can cause this anemia. By delivering iron together with folic acid and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, fatty acids, and supportive agents, IRON/MULTIVITAMINS aim to correct iron‑deficiency anemia, prevent or treat folate‑deficiency‑related macrocytic anemia, and address overall nutritional needs, thereby providing a comprehensive micronutrient therapy for patients at risk of hematologic and nutritional disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate are both members of the therapeutic class identified as iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations. This class comprises oral formulations that deliver trivalent iron to patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, thereby replenishing depleted iron stores and supporting hemoglobin synthesis. By belonging to the same category, ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate share a common mechanism of action and are used interchangeably as oral options for correcting iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is a specific form of فقر الدم (anemia) that arises rapidly after substantial blood loss, leading to a sudden decrease in circulating red blood cells and hemoglobin levels. As a subtype of anemia, it shares the fundamental characteristic of reduced oxygen‑carrying capacity of the blood, but its acute onset distinguishes it from chronic or nutritional anemias. Prompt recognition and treatment of acute posthemorrhagic anemia are essential in patient care, as rapid restoration of intravascular volume and red cell mass—through fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, and control of the bleeding source—are required to prevent tissue hypoxia, organ dysfunction, and hemodynamic instability. Understanding that acute posthemorrhagic anemia falls under the broader category of فقر الدم helps clinicians classify the condition, anticipate its pathophysiology, and apply appropriate therapeutic strategies within the overall framework of anemia management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1384
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as a form of “Other deficiency anemias.” The category of Other deficiency anemias encompasses several distinct types, including فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia), and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Recognizing that anemia associated with chronic kidney disease falls within this broader group helps clinicians consider the underlying nutritional or hemolytic mechanisms that may contribute to the patient’s low hemoglobin and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted treatment strategies.

Collectively, these multivitamin‑iron and zinc products aim to improve hematopoiesis, correct simultaneous micronutrient deficiencies, enhance iron absorption (often facilitated by vitamin C and chelated iron forms), reduce gastrointestinal side effects, and provide comprehensive metabolic support for patients at risk of iron‑deficiency anemia, folate‑deficiency anemia, combined nutritional deficits, or those requiring bariatric supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2003
retrieved_docs.content: Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations are classified as members of the sodium ironedetate group, indicating that sodium ironedetate is a specific type of iron trivalent oral antianemic medication used to treat iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2281
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an Other deficiency anemia. The category Other deficiency anemias includes فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Recognizing that anemia in chronic kidney disease falls within this broader group of deficiency‑related anemias helps clinicians consider overlapping etiologies, appropriate diagnostic work‑up, and targeted therapeutic strategies.
